summary: Ronacher argues that while async/await in Python exposed more developers to concurrency, it also imposed complex machinery, colored functions, and leaky abstractions. With free-threading on the horizon, the neat guarantee of async suspension points erodes, leaving developers to juggle both async and thread complexity. He proposes re-centering concurrency on threads—specifically virtual threads—paired with structured concurrency so the runtime, not user code, carries most of the complexity. He sketches a “better API” that keeps blocking-style code while enabling structured parallelism, showing how this could avoid futures for most common cases. He concludes that the idea is a conversation starter, but the direction—virtual threads plus structured concurrency—could make async/await largely unnecessary for future code.
key_points: Async/await broadened access to concurrency but forced complex, leaky semantics like colored functions into Python’s surface API.
Free-threading makes async’s “only suspend at await” guarantee less meaningful, pushing Python toward a thread-first model.
Structured concurrency is a major ergonomic win, but its cancellation guarantees break down when thread pools or blocking I/O are involved.
Virtual threads could restore a simple blocking programming model while keeping structured concurrency and performance.
Complexity should move into the runtime (locking, cancellation, scheduling), letting programmers avoid futures and promises for most tasks.
money_quotes: Async/await did actually produce one very good outcome for Python: it has exposed many more people to concurrent programming.
Unfortunately, recent changes with free-threading make that guarantee rather pointless.
This is a good moment to rethink if we maybe have a better path in front of us by fully embracing threads.
In many ways, what we really want is to go back to the drawing board and say, "What does a world look like that only ever would have used threads with a better API?"
What is so important about this entire concept is that it moves a lot of the complexity of concurrent programming where it belongs: into the interpreter and the internal APIs.
Even though it could support futures, you can actually express a whole lot of programming code without needing to defer to an abstraction like that.
The idea of no longer having to deal with colored functions really appeals to me and I hope we can explore it a bit.
key_insight: Virtual threads plus structured concurrency could let Python keep a simple blocking style while moving concurrency complexity into the runtime, making async/await largely unnecessary.
